justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — California wage transparency as legal requirement (BR-3), Germany works council as blocking precondition (BR-4), Indonesia as no legal mandate (BR-8). (2) Germany Betriebsrat approval explicitly identified as invariant precondition with legal citation (BetrVG §87). (3) Driver classification data model difference clearly identified in BR-5 and domain analysis (employee in CA vs contractor in ID, unknown in DE). (4) Formal escalation for legal review present — three structured escalations including blocking German classification until legal confirms. (5) No single global acceptance criteria set — ACs are jurisdiction-specific (AC-1 for CA, AC-2/AC-3 for DE, AC-4 for ID). (6) confidence.level = 52, well under the 60 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough jurisdiction-specific analysis with separate business rules per jurisdiction, not a single global rule set
Germany works council (Betriebsrat) correctly identified as invariant legal gate with specific law citation (BetrVG §87) and edge case for approval revocation
Driver classification difference clearly articulated as a legal fact consumed by the system, never set by it (BR-5)
Confidence level of 52 appropriately reflects legal complexity and unresolved ambiguities
Escalation explicitly recommends blocking German requirements until classification is confirmed by legal counsel — strong formal escalation
Identified the subtle trap that Indonesia transparency is a business choice (invariant: false) while California is legal mandate (invariant: true), with clear justification for each
Edge case for Betriebsrat approval revocation post-rollout shows deep understanding of the Germany requirement lifecycle
Business impact correctly identifies phased jurisdiction-specific rollout as necessary and quantifies California legal penalties
Rich ubiquitous language including German-specific terms (Betriebsrat) shows domain depth

Could have more explicitly called out the asymmetric removal risk — that removing transparency in Indonesia is fine but removing it in California would be illegal — though this is partially captured in the invariant distinctions

grader_results.detail: field escalations[*].type equals check failed: expected 'ambiguity', got values: 'missing_context', 'missing_context'
